questions.question: Explain the three names of a shared library and their purposes.
keyPoints: Linker name (libfoo.so) - used at compile time for linking
SONAME (libfoo.so.1) - embedded in executable, used at runtime for loading
Real name (libfoo.so.1.0.0) - actual file with complete version

questions.question: What is the difference between static and shared libraries? Give two advantages of shared libraries.
keyPoints: Static: code copied into executable at compile time. Shared: code loaded at runtime from external file.
Advantages: 1) Reduced executable size 2) Reduced memory usage (single copy in RAM) 3) Updates without recompilation 4) Faster linking at compile time

questions.question: Explain the purpose of LD_LIBRARY_PATH and ldconfig.
keyPoints: LD_LIBRARY_PATH: Environment variable specifying directories to search for shared libraries at runtime
ldconfig: Utility that updates the shared library cache (/etc/ld.so.cache) by scanning directories and creating proper symbolic links

questions.question: What is Position Independent Code (PIC) and why is it required for shared libraries?
keyPoints: PIC is machine code that can execute correctly regardless of its absolute memory address
Required because shared libraries can be loaded at different addresses in different programs
Without PIC, the library would need relocation processing for each program

questions.question: Explain lazy binding and the difference between RTLD_LAZY and RTLD_NOW.
keyPoints: Lazy binding resolves symbols only when first called, not at program startup
RTLD_LAZY: Deferred binding - faster startup, errors found later
RTLD_NOW: Immediate binding - all symbols resolved at load time, slower startup but detects errors early

questions.question: Describe the dynamic library loading functions dlopen() and dlsym().
keyPoints: dlopen(): Loads a shared library explicitly at runtime, returns a handle
dlsym(): Finds the address of a symbol (function/variable) in a loaded library using the handle
Together they allow programs to load functionality dynamically without linking at compile time

questions.question: Explain the complete process of creating and using shared libraries in Linux, including compilation, linking, and runtime loading.
keyPoints: 1. Creating: gcc -fPIC -c foo.c → gcc -shared -o libfoo.so foo.o
2. Installing: Copy to /usr/lib or set LD_LIBRARY_PATH
3. Running ldconfig for system-wide libraries
4. Compiling with library: gcc -o program main.c -lfoo (-L/path -Wl,-rpath=/path)
5. Runtime: Dynamic linker (ld.so) loads library based on embedded path or search paths
6. Symbol resolution: Maps function names to addresses
7. Execution: Program uses shared code from memory

questions.question: Compare static and shared libraries in detail, explaining the advantages and disadvantages of each approach.
keyPoints: Static Libraries: Code copied at compile time, larger executables, no runtime dependency, faster execution
Shared Libraries: Code loaded at runtime, smaller executables, runtime dependency required, slightly slower due to indirection
Advantages of Shared: Reduced disk/RAM usage, easy updates, faster compile time, flexibility
Disadvantages of Shared: DLL hell (version issues), runtime dependency, slight performance overhead, compatibility concerns
Use static for: Embedded systems, standalone executables, performance-critical applications

questions.question: Explain the Linux shared library search mechanism. Include LD_PRELOAD, LD_LIBRARY_PATH, /etc/ld.so.conf, and /etc/ld.so.cache in your answer.
keyPoints: Search Order: 1) LD_PRELOAD (user-specified override) 2) LD_LIBRARY_PATH (user-specified paths) 3) Binary's RPATH 4) /etc/ld.so.cache 5) Default (/lib, /usr/lib)
LD_PRELOAD: Libraries to load before all others - can override functions
LD_LIBRARY_PATH: User-defined search directories at runtime
/etc/ld.so.conf: Configuration file listing library directories
ldconfig: Creates /etc/ld.so.cache binary from /etc/ld.so.conf for fast lookups
RPATH: Embedded path in executable set at compile time with -Wl,-rpath=
